llama-3.1-8b-instant.fix_testing_first: Adding frontend tests might uncover issues that prevent shipping to production.
llama-3.1-8b-instant.fix_security_first: Over-engineering for 0 users might incur additional costs.
llama-3.1-8b-instant.fix_api_client_first: OpenAPI schema gaps may require manual overrides.
llama-3.1-8b-instant.ship_first_fix_later: Security incident with real user data is a high risk.
llama-3.1-8b-instant.prop_0: Proposed path may not address all security concerns.
llama-3.1-8b-instant.prop_1: Generated types might not cover all API client use cases.
llama-3.1-8b-instant.prop_2: Proposed path may not address all security concerns.

meta-llama/llama-4-scout-17b-16e-instruc.fix_testing_first: May delay feature development for 2 weeks with uncertain immediate benefits.
meta-llama/llama-4-scout-17b-16e-instruc.fix_security_first: Adds $5-10/mo cost for Redis/Sentry, potentially exceeding the $50/mo budget.
meta-llama/llama-4-scout-17b-16e-instruc.fix_api_client_first: Generated types may be incomplete if OpenAPI schema has gaps.
meta-llama/llama-4-scout-17b-16e-instruc.ship_first_fix_later: Security incidents or regressions possible without proper testing and security measures.
meta-llama/llama-4-scout-17b-16e-instruc.prop_0: May not fully address security concerns, such as rate limiting and monitoring.
meta-llama/llama-4-scout-17b-16e-instruc.prop_1: Relies on Supabase Edge Functions for rate limiting, which may have limitations.
meta-llama/llama-4-scout-17b-16e-instruc.prop_2: Quick hardening measures may not be comprehensive or long-term solutions.

llama-3.3-70b-versatile.fix_testing_first: Insufficient testing may lead to undiscovered bugs and regressions.
llama-3.3-70b-versatile.fix_security_first: Security gaps could be exploited by malicious actors, especially with a single instance and no DevOps support.
llama-3.3-70b-versatile.fix_api_client_first: Type safety issues may cause errors and slow down development.
llama-3.3-70b-versatile.ship_first_fix_later: Shipping without proper testing and security measures could result in a security incident or data breach.

gemini-flash-lite-latest.fix_testing_first: Massive effort (10-14 days) yields zero immediate user/client value, violating the pragmatist need to ship quickly for the 2-week Pasha Bank pitch.
gemini-flash-lite-latest.fix_security_first: Introducing Redis adds recurring cost ($5-10/mo) and complexity (new external dependency), potentially breaking the tight $50/mo budget cushion, and development time is high.
gemini-flash-lite-latest.fix_api_client_first: While developer friction is high, type mismatches are less likely to cause a catastrophic, public failure than the known in-memory rate limiter issue during a high-volume demo.
gemini-flash-lite-latest.ship_first_fix_later: Shipping the in-memory rate limiter as-is guarantees failure/security exposure if the demo/initial load spikes above Railway's restart frequency or concurrency limit.
gemini-flash-lite-latest.prop_0: Reliance on a single agent to define 'basic monitoring' for a bank demo is risky; it might miss the critical security gaps identified (like the in-memory limit).
gemini-flash-lite-latest.prop_1: Implementing a Supabase Edge Function rate limiter is complex (requires learning Edge Functions/Deno runtime) and might take longer than the estimated 1-2 days, delaying the demo.
gemini-flash-lite-latest.prop_2: CSP hardening and prompt stripping are excellent security hygiene but do nothing to address the known, immediate scaling/reliability issue of the in-memory rate limiter.

openai/gpt-oss-120b.fix_testing_first: Testing infrastructure adds no protection; a bank demo could expose raw JWTs and RLS misconfigurations, leading to data breach.
openai/gpt-oss-120b.fix_security_first: Adding Redis for rate limiting introduces an external point of failure and $5‑10/mo cost that may exceed the $50 budget, leaving no cushion for other services.
openai/gpt-oss-120b.fix_api_client_first: Generated OpenAPI client may omit custom auth handling, causing JWT mismatches and potential unauthorized data access.
openai/gpt-oss-120b.ship_first_fix_later: In‑memory rate limiter and no monitoring mean a spike to 5,000 concurrent users will crash the single Railway instance, causing denial‑of‑service and data loss.

moonshotai/kimi-k2-instruct.fix_testing_first: Zero tests today means a CI pipeline would be red for weeks—time you don’t have before the Pasha Bank demo.
moonshotai/kimi-k2-instruct.fix_security_first: Redis at $5–10/mo plus Sentry leaves only ~$35 for everything else; one traffic spike and you’re over budget.
moonshotai/kimi-k2-instruct.fix_api_client_first: Generated types can’t enforce RLS: if the OpenAPI spec omits hidden_verification_status, frontend still builds but queries 403.
moonshotai/kimi-k2-instruct.ship_first_fix_later: In-memory rate limiter resets on every Railway restart—an attacker can script 1000 login attempts in <60s and own the demo account.
moonshotai/kimi-k2-instruct.prop_0: Supabase Edge Functions have 10s CPU cap; a burst of 1000 malicious requests can still exhaust the limiter and starve legitimate traffic.
moonshotai/kimi-k2-instruct.prop_1: Edge-function rate limiter shares the same 10s timeout; a determined attacker can rotate IPs and still bypass the limit.
moonshotai/kimi-k2-instruct.prop_2: CSP report-only mode is noise until someone reads the reports—founder has no alerting, so injected scripts still run silently.

gemini-2.5-flash-lite.fix_testing_first: Significant time investment with no immediate user-facing benefit, and no guarantee of preventing critical bugs before the bank demo.
gemini-2.5-flash-lite.fix_security_first: Over-engineering for 0 users might strain the $50/mo budget, especially if Redis is chosen, and delays critical features needed for the demo.
gemini-2.5-flash-lite.fix_api_client_first: While it improves dev speed, it doesn't address the more critical security vulnerabilities or the immediate need for a hardened system for the bank demo.
gemini-2.5-flash-lite.ship_first_fix_later: Shipping with known security gaps (in-memory rate limiter, no monitoring) to a bank is an unacceptable risk that could lead to immediate data breaches or denial of service.
gemini-2.5-flash-lite.prop_0: This path focuses on specific security issues but might not be comprehensive enough for a bank demo, and the reliance on Redis could push the budget.
gemini-2.5-flash-lite.prop_1: This path effectively balances immediate development velocity with crucial security hardening without exceeding the budget, addressing both the API client and rate limiting using free tiers.
gemini-2.5-flash-lite.prop_2: While quick and zero-cost, these security measures are more preventative and might not be sufficient to satisfy the immediate security concerns for a B2B client demo.

moonshotai/kimi-k2-instruct-0905.fix_testing_first: Zero frontend tests means UI regressions will silently break the assessment flow; without a CI gate you'll ship broken states to a bank demo.
moonshotai/kimi-k2-instruct-0905.fix_security_first: Redis at $5-10/mo is 10-20% of your entire runway—when the single Railway dyno restarts you still lose the limiter state; Supabase Edge Functions do the job for free.
moonshotai/kimi-k2-instruct-0905.fix_api_client_first: The OpenAPI spec omits RLS-protected fields (e.g., hidden_verification_status) so generated types will compile but still crash at runtime when Supabase returns 403.
moonshotai/kimi-k2-instruct-0905.ship_first_fix_later: No CSP + LLM-evaluated open answers = prompt-injection XSS that could exfiltrate JWTs; Pasha Bank's security team will kill the deal on first pen-test.
moonshotai/kimi-k2-instruct-0905.prop_0: Supabase Edge Functions have 10s CPU/150 MB limits; at 5,000 concurrent your rate-limit logic will timeout and drop legitimate traffic.
moonshotai/kimi-k2-instruct-0905.prop_1: Edge-rate-limit + generated types still leave the LLM prompt-injection vector unpatched; Gemini will cheerfully echo back malicious JS into the DOM.
moonshotai/kimi-k2-instruct-0905.prop_2: Report-only CSP won't block anything; strip-tags is bypassed with Unicode homoglyphs; 1-shot prompt guard is trivially jail-broken by nesting instructions.

deepseek-chat.fix_testing_first: Spending 2 weeks on testing infrastructure when there's a bank demo in 2 weeks and zero frontend tests means you'll have no visible progress for the demo while critical security and type safety issues remain unaddressed.
deepseek-chat.fix_security_first: Adding Redis ($5-10/mo) on a $50/mo budget leaves little room for other essential costs, and the in-memory rate limiter is only a problem at scale - you have 0 users now and a single instance.
deepseek-chat.fix_api_client_first: OpenAPI schema gaps could make generated types incomplete, requiring manual overrides that negate the time savings, and this doesn't address the immediate security concerns for a bank demo.
deepseek-chat.ship_first_fix_later: Deploying with in-memory rate limiting, no frontend tests, and manual API types to a bank client demo risks immediate security incidents and broken assessment flows that could kill the deal.
deepseek-chat.prop_0: Fixing only 'demo-critical' security issues leaves other security gaps (like CSP headers, prompt injection protection) unaddressed, and Redis cost still impacts the tight budget.
deepseek-chat.prop_1: Supabase Edge Functions for rate limiting is unproven at scale (5,000 concurrent users), and the free tier may have limitations that could break under load during the bank demo.
deepseek-chat.prop_2: CSP report-only mode and prompt guards don't address the fundamental rate limiting vulnerability or type safety issues - it's security theater without addressing the actual attack vectors.

innovations.llama-3.1-8b-instant: Implementing a hybrid security demo to address critical security gaps without breaking budget.
innovations.meta-llama/llama-4-scout-17b-16e-instruc: Using Supabase Edge Functions for rate limiting, combining security and development speed improvements.
innovations.llama-3.3-70b-versatile: Implement a chaos engineering approach to simulate failures and test the system's resilience.
innovations.gemini-flash-lite-latest: To eliminate the Redis cost concern and maintain the single-instance architecture, use a hardened, persistent rate limiting strategy within the Railway application itself (e.g., using a file-backed key-value store if Railway supports persistent storage, or using a shared secret/JWT claim for rate limits if possible, though Redis is still the cleanest fix for the in-memory problem).
innovations.openai/gpt-oss-120b: Implement a lightweight Supabase Edge function that performs rate‑limiting and vector‑search throttling, eliminating the need for a separate Redis instance and staying within budget.
innovations.moonshotai/kimi-k2-instruct: Wrap every Gemini call with a 2-second client-side timeout and a fallback canned response; if the LLM hangs, the assessment still completes and you keep the demo alive.
innovations.gemini-2.5-flash-lite: Leveraging Supabase Edge Functions for rate limiting offers a cost-effective and scalable solution that aligns with the existing infrastructure, avoiding the need for a separate Redis instance and its associated costs and complexity.
innovations.moonshotai/kimi-k2-instruct-0905: Wrap every LLM-bound user string with a tiny frontend WASM module that ROT13-encodes it before transit and decodes server-side after Gemini returns; this neuters prompt-injection payloads without touching CSP or prompt engineering.
innovations.deepseek-chat: Implement a hybrid rate limiter: use in-memory for normal traffic but fall back to a simple SQL-based counter in Supabase when thresholds are approached, avoiding Redis cost while maintaining basic protection.
